MOVING PARTY FAILED TO CARRY ITS BURDEN UNDER THE ANTI-SLAPP STATUTE. DEFENDANTS APPEAR TO SEEK TO STRIKE THE ENTIRE COMPLAINT (SEE NOTICE OF MOTION) AND NOT ANY SPECIFIC CLAIMS ARISING OUT OF PROTECTED ACTIVITIES. FURTHERMORE, MOVING PARTY FAILS TO SHOW THAT THE CLAIMS ARISE SOLELY OUT OF THE SERVICE OF THE 60 DAY NOTICE, I.E., THAT THE SERVICE ITSELF IS THE WRONG COMPLAINED ABOUT BY THE PLAINTIFFS. ADDITIONALLY, ASSUMING THAT SOME OF THE CLAIMS IN THE COMPLAINT ARISE OUT OF PROTECTED ACTIVITY, PLAINTIFFS PROVIDE SUFFICIENT EVIDENCE TO DEMONSTRATE PROBABILITY OF PREVAILING ON THE MERITS. THE COURT ALSO NOTES THAT THE MOVING PARTY APPEARS TO SEEK A DETERMINATION OF THE PROPRIETY OF ALLEGATIONS ADMITTEDLY NOT ARISING OUT OF PROTECTED ACTIVITY (E.G. HABITABILITY) ON THIS MOTION. NO AUTHORITY IS CITED FOR THE PROPOSITION THAT THE ANTI-SLAPP IS A PROPER MOTION TO CHALLENGE SUCH ALLEGATIONS. PLAINTIFFS TO PREPARE ORDER IN COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312(B). JUDGE: CHARLES F. Defendants contest the court's tentative ruling. Ms. Alexandra contests the court's tentative ruling with regard to plaintiff's request for attorney fees raised in their opposition. Plaintiffs argue that defendants' motion is frivolous. After reviewing the pleadings and listening to oral arguments the court makes a finding that defendants' motion is not frivolous and adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS BIANA CHERNOGUZ AND VLADISLAV CHERNOGUZ'S NOTICE OF MOTION TO STRIKE NEW COMPLAINT AS UNLAWFUL S.L.A.P.P. SUIT is DENIED. Moving party failed to carry its burden under the ANTI-SLAPP statute. Defendants appear to seek to strike the entire complaint (See Notice of Motion) and not any specific claims arising out of protected activities. Furthermore, moving party fails to show that the claims arise solely out of the service of the 60 Day Notice, i.e., that the service itself is the wrong complained about by the Plaintiffs. Additionally, assuming that some of the claims in the complaint arise out of protected activity, Plaintiffs provide sufficient evidence to demonstrate probability of prevailing on the merits. The Court also notes that the moving party appears to seek a determination of the propriety of allegations admittedly not arising out of protected activity (e.g. habitability) on this motion. No authority is cited for the proposition that the ANTI-SLAPP is a proper motion to challenge such allegations.
